coredata.dc:title: Efficient visible light-driven Photodegradation of malachite green dye using carbon quantum dots-MXene nanocomposite: Synthesis, characterization, and performance evaluation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:issueName: Investigation on the effect of wastewater on ecosystems and development of promising technologies for wastewater treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Zahedi, Mir Mahdi
Samarin, Somayeh Keyvani
Abkenar, Shiva Dehghan
Pebdeni, Azam Bagheri
Hosseini, Morteza
Kalikeri, Shankramma
Karimi-Maleh, Hassan
coredata.dc:description:
Background Removal of azo dyes is one of important issue in water treatment studies and many of research works focused on it. Current work represents a simple and economical feasible to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) integrated with Ti3C2(OH)2 MXene nanocomposite (CQDs/MXene) for rapid photodegradation of Malachite green (MG) under visible light irradiation. In practice, CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method from Petroselinum crispum leaves, while Ti3C2(OH)2 nanosheets resulted from a simple etching process of Ti3AlC2 using an alkaline solution (KOH with a trace amount of water).
Methods Subsequently, nanocomposites (CQDs/MXene) were prepared by a self-assembly process through sonication-assisted mixing process under ambient conditions. The prepared catalysts were characterized using various analyses like analysis employing field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and an energy dispersion X-ray (EDX) spectrometer to determine the chemical composition. The optimum measurement of this activity of the CQDs/MXene was attained through adjusting several constraints such as pH, dye concentration, amount of temperature and photocatalyst.
Significant findings The kinetics of the degradation process were represented using the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model, with a rate constant (k) of 0.1382 min−1. This means that the degradation of MG dye follows a first-order reaction with respect to the dye concentration. In addition, the study also looked at the reusability of the prepared CQDs/MXene nanophotocatalyst and found that it exhibited outstanding stability after three reaction cycles. This suggests that the nanophotocatalyst is a promising material for efficient and sustainable degradation of MG dye. The nanocomposite displays high photocatalytic activity with the ability to reduce 96.1 % of the MG dye concentration after being exposed to 25 min of visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Dyes
Carbon quantum dots
MXene
Composite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance of CQDs/MXene nanocomposites in photo-catalysis
Effects of the initial concentration of dye
Effect of irradiation time on photocatalysis reaction
Kinetic study of the MG degradation
Proposed mechanism for superior photocatalytic activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) SEM images of MXene (left) and nanocomposite (right), b) XRD patterns, c) EDX elemental mapping of nanocomposite, and d) the FTIR spectrum of CQD, MXene and CQDs/MXene.
a) The images of the MG dye solution before (left) and after (right) show the photodegradation of MG by MXene, CQDs, and CQDs/MXene nanocomposites after 30 min of exposure to visible light.
a) Effect of pH, b) Effect of amount of CQDs/MXene nanocomposite, c) Effect of initial concentration of dye, and d) irradiation time on the photocatalysis reaction under visible light irradiation.
a) Degradation percent, b) Ln C/Co versus time of MG dye in presence of CQDs, MXene and CQDs/MXene nanocomposite under visible light irradiation.
The reusability tests of MXene/CQDs photocatalyst for three cycles of MG degradation.
An illustration of the photocatalytic pathway for the degradation of MG by CQDs/MXene nanocomposite.
Comparison of MG degradation over different photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Efficient visible light-driven Photodegradation of malachite green dye using carbon quantum dots-MXene nanocomposite: Synthesis, characterization, and performance evaluation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Removal of azo dyes is one of important issue in water treatment studies and many of research works focused on it. Current work represents a simple and economical feasible to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) integrated with Ti3C2(OH)2 MXene nanocomposite (CQDs/MXene) for rapid photodegradation of Malachite green (MG) under visible light irradiation. In practice, CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method from Petroselinum crispum leaves, while Ti3C2(OH)2 nanosheets resulted from a simple etching process of Ti3AlC2 using an alkaline solution (KOH with a trace amount of water).

Subsequently, nanocomposites (CQDs/MXene) were prepared by a self-assembly process through sonication-assisted mixing process under ambient conditions. The prepared catalysts were characterized using various analyses like analysis employing field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and an energy dispersion X-ray (EDX) spectrometer to determine the chemical composition. The optimum measurement of this activity of the CQDs/MXene was attained through adjusting several constraints such as pH, dye concentration, amount of temperature and photocatalyst.

The kinetics of the degradation process were represented using the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model, with a rate constant (k) of 0.1382 min−1. This means that the degradation of MG dye follows a first-order reaction with respect to the dye concentration. In addition, the study also looked at the reusability of the prepared CQDs/MXene nanophotocatalyst and found that it exhibited outstanding stability after three reaction cycles. This suggests that the nanophotocatalyst is a promising material for efficient and sustainable degradation of MG dye. The nanocomposite displays high photocatalytic activity with the ability to reduce 96.1 % of the MG dye concentration after being exposed to 25 min of visible light.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent world, water pollution due to organic compounds has emerged as a global concern. The removal of pollutants is becoming an important environmental issue due to their wide application in the activities of many industrial plants, including textile, dye factories, food manufacturers, and plating plants. These industries release effluents containing dyes and thus pollute water resources [1]. Dyes are an important class of pollutants, the majority of which exhibit stability and resistance to oxidizing agents, photodegradation, and biodegradation [2]. Transforming organic pollutants into CO2 and H2O through artificial photosynthesis stands out as a highly promising strategy for wastewater treatment. This approach is notable for its exceptional efficiency, safety, and reliance on abundant sunlight [5]

Carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) have become a subject of significant interest in photocatalysis because of their plentiful presence, non-toxicity, affordability, and extended lifespan [3,4]. Furthermore, the distinctive characteristics of CQDs, such as their exceptional aqueous solubility, chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, and ease of production and customization, have distinguished them from conventional semiconductor quantum dots [5].

MXene is a 2D material composed of transition metal carbides and/or nitrides, which has been derived from MAX phases. This innovative material exhibits a broad spectrum of various uses, such as but not restricted to supercapacitors, hydrogen storage, lithium-ion batteries, water purification, and electromagnetic absorption [6–14]. Few- or monolayer Ti3C2Tx MXene has attracted much attention among this group of materials as it exposes a greater number of reaction sites and termination groups. Surface terminating groups are activated through deliberate surface ornamentation of few-layer Ti3C2Tx MXene can be further induced [15–17].

The potential applications of CQDs have greatly expanded since their discovery, including chemical sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, and photocatalysis [18–23]. In the field of visible-light-driven heterogeneous photocatalysis, CQDs have received significant attention due to their remarkable aqueous solubility, efficient electron transfer, and exceptional light-harvesting ability, allowing for full utilization of the solar spectrum. In particular, green CQDs have recently garnered interest in this field due to their non-toxic nature, dispersibility, high compatibility, and excellent chemical stability [5,24]. Additionally, the CQDs possess various functional groups, including -COOH and -OH, located at the periphery of their structure that exhibit remarkable react with other active substances. For instance, CQDs can be conveniently synthesized with NiO [3] (metal oxides) and Ni(OH)2 (metal hydroxides) [25]. This exceptional affinity of CQDs offers promising potential to utilize them for surface modification of Ti3C2Tx MXene to develop a high-performance composite material of CQDs/MXene [15].

The azo dyes are a significant group of organic compounds with a variety of both scientific and industrial applications [26,27]. The controversial use of MG, a triarylmethane dye, in fisheries for treating and preventing fungal and protozoa infections. Despite its effectiveness, low cost, and widespread availability, MG is organized by the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA, USA) has classified it as a class II health hazard due to its potential to be carcinogenic, mutagenic, teratogenic, and toxic. For example, the LC50 value for MG in carp is 0.438 mg/L (96 h), and its lethality increases with exposure time. Therefore, MG has been prohibited in numerous countries for many years, including the United States, Japan, and the European Union [28].

In this work, we synthesized hydroxyl (OH)-terminated MXene (Ti3C2Tx) using Ti3AlC2 as precursors through the method outlined in previous literature, using the KOH solution to achieve a straightforward approach [29]. Then, the obtained Ti3C2Tx MXene was modified with produced CQDs to prepare an efficient photocatalyst. MG, a cationic dye, was employed as a model compound to assess the photocatalytic activity of prepared nanocomposites under visible light irradiation (Scheme 1 ). The CQDs utilized in this research were obtained through an environmentally friendly approach employing Petroselinum crispum leaves, demonstrating our commitment to green chemistry practices. Our findings show that the resulting powder has excellent photocatalytic abilities, proven through consistent performance over three cycles of photodegradation. Notably, it achieved remarkable degradation of Malachite green dye after just 25 min of visible light exposure. The outstanding photocatalytic activity of the produced nanocomposites was achieved by evaluating the dye degradation efficiency 96.1 % and rate constant (k) 0.1382 min−1 of CQDs/MXene nanocomposites on MG.

MG, Ti, Al, graphite powders, potassium dichromate and potassium hydroxide were purchased from Merck No additional purification was performed on any of the compounds before their usage; they were all of analytical grade. DDW, or double-distilled water, was used throughout the entire trial. The necessary quantity of MG was dissolved in DDW to create stock solutions for the dye. A Lab 850 Benchtop pH meter was used to measure each pH value. For UV–visible spectrum analyses, a Perkin Elmer (Lambda 25) UV-visible spectrometer was used. The morphological properties of the nanocomposite were studied using SEM (TESCAN MIRA III) with Cu (= 1.5456) and scan rate 5° min 1 (steps: 0.05°). A powder X-ray diffractometer (PHILIPS PW1730 XRD) was used to assess the crystallinity of prepared samples. To determine the constituent elements and abundance distribution of the elements that were present in the sample, the MAP method (TESCAN MIRA II) was used.

The CQDs were prepared through a green chemistry procedure. For this, unique CQDs were fabricated using Petroselinum crispum plant via hydrothermal method. Initially add 5 g of fresh Petroselinum crispum leaves and wash them. After adding 20 ml double distilled water, the solution was added into 50 mL steel autoclave, and hydrothermal reaction was performed at 180 °C for 12 h. Eventually, the final product, a brown color solution, was extracted. Pure CQDs were obtained by filtration using 0.22 µm syringe filter and centrifuge the solution.

To synthesize Ti3ALC2, a precise mixture of Ti, Al, and graphite powders was prepared with a stoichiometric ratio of 3:1:2, respectively. The total mass of the mixture is 10 g, ensuring that the proportions are maintained accurately to achieve the desired composition. The prepared mixture was transferred to a steel ball mill, where mechanical milling is employed to homogenize the powder. The ball mill operated at 450 rpm for an extended duration of 18 h. This milling process helps in reducing particle size, enhancing reactivity, and ensuring uniform distribution of the components. Approximately 8 g of the milled mixture were heated at 1000 °C for a duration of 3 h in a tube furnace. The tube furnace is operated under an argon gas flow, creating an inert atmosphere.

The synthesis process was based on a one-step method, following the methodology detailed in a published article [19]. The initial mixture consisted of potassium hydroxide (KOH) weighing 7.0 g, Ti3AlC2 weighing 1.0 g, and a small amount of water (1 mL). These components were carefully combined to form a paste-like consistency. The prepared paste was then transferred to an autoclave constructed from stainless steel and lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). The autoclave was subjected to a heating process for a duration of 24 h. The temperature was maintained at 160 °C, which is a critical factor influencing the kinetics and outcome of the reaction. After the completion of the 24 h reaction, the solid product was collected from the autoclave. The resulting material was then subjected to a series of washing steps with deionized distilled water (DDW). This washing process continued until the pH of the filtrate reached a neutral value of 7. Following the washing steps, a vacuum oven was employed to dry the collected sample. The drying process occurred at a temperature of 70 °C and continued for 12 h, ensuring the removal of residual water and any volatile components. The synthesized Ti3C2(OH)2 product can be characterized using various analytical techniques, such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR).

To prepare a few-layer Ti3C2Tx, the typical procedure involves etching bulk Ti3AlC2 with a solution that contains KOH and little water. The exfoliation of the bulk material results in the formation of a single crystalline sheet. Remarkably, the exfoliating process incorporates numerous terminating groups onto the Ti3C2Tx surface, which function as active sites for securing active material to the Ti3C2Tx matrix material. Simultaneously, CQDs were obtained by hydrothermal treatment using Petroselinum crispum leaves as the carbon source. Through a conventional self-assembly process, the Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets, which possess a significant number of terminating groups, undergo a covalent cross-linking reaction with CQDs to form CQDs/MXene composite.

Degradation of MG by photocatalysis, this dye was evaluated with a homemade photochemical reactor. Required amount of CQDs/MXene was dispersed in dye solution (75 ppm) and was kept at room temperature for 25 min. After that, it was placed under visible light for 25 min to monitor the photodegradation. A halogen lamp (FTL MR11 220v 35 W) was used for visible light irradiation. The photocatalysts were dispersed in 10 ml of MG solution at pH 6 and stirred continuously using a magnetic stirrer in the dark at room temperature for 25 min to attain the adsorption-desorption equilibrium state. During the irradiation process, 5 ml of the thoroughly mixed suspension was extracted at specific time intervals and then centrifuged for 10 min to determine the concentration of residual MG dyes using a UV–Vis spectrometer at a particular wavelength of 617 nm. Given the data, the dye degradation efficiency was determined using the following equation (Eq.1):
(1)
% R =
[
c o
−
c t
c o
]
× 100

In which Co and Ct are the initial MG concentration and the final MG concentration at time (t) (mgL−1). Additionally, the chemical oxygen demand (COD) was measured applying potassium dichromate standard method to effectively determine the mineralization degree of organic dye. The effects of the initial dye concentration, pH, and amount of photocatalyst on degradation efficiency were examined. The evaluation of the contact time and kinetic analysis were also carried out.

Fig. 1 a shows representative FE-SEM images of MXene (left) and nanocomposite (right). The images showed compact, sheet and layered morphology of MXene, which did not alter after preparation of CQDs/MXene composite. Phase purity and composition of the as-prepared CQDs/MXene were examined by XRD. The prominent peaks were seen around 2θ = 36, 39, 42, 53, 60, and 72. The amorphous structure of CQDs introduces some noise in the data [3]. The XRD spectrum of MXene, after washing and drying, displayed strong diffraction peaks at approximately 38° and 40°, which correspond to MAX and have disappeared, proving the formation of Ti3C2(OH)2 MXene (as shown in Fig. 1b). This result is consistent with previous reports on the XRD diffraction peaks of layered Ti3C2(OH)2 structure [29]. The presence of a titanium phase was also corroborated by the XRD pattern of CQDs/MXene. XRD analysis of MXene synthesized through hydrothermal method revealed a broad diffraction peak at 28°, suggesting the presence of Ti3C2Tx. The sharp peaks observed in the XRD patterns of the MXene catalyst indicate that the nanocomposite synthesized is well-crystallized.

Energy dispersive analysis of X-rays (EDX) was employed to examine the composite. The EDX spectra verified the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, aluminum, and titanium elements with an abundance of 30, 23.89, 16.99, 6.72, and 22.40, respectively (Fig. 1c).

The FTIR spectrum of CQDs, MXene and CQDs/MXene nanocomposites are shown in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 (Fig. 1d). The FTIR spectrum of CQDs contains the main peaks located at 3312 cm−1 (OH stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups) and 1280 cm−1 (CNHC symmetric stretching). The absorbance bands at 1400 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of CN and CO, respectively and at 1638.4 cm−1 related to CO symmetric stretching. The FTIR spectrum of MXene has a peak found at 3475.2 cm−1 (OH stretching vibration) and the peaks at 554 and 647 cm−1, corresponding to a Ti− O stretching of MXene. CQDs/MXene composite also has a peak of 1574.4 cm−1 (C = O stretching vibration). [3,30,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CQDs/MXene nanocomposites in photo-catalysis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs, MXene, and CQDs/MXene nanocomposites under visible light exposure, MG was employed as the contaminant of interest. Suspension of reactions were made by adding 6 mg of the composite catalyst to 25–200 ppm of MG solution. To achieve absorption-desorption equilibrium, the aqueous suspensions were stirred without exposure to light for 30 min. Subsequently, they were agitated for 25 min while under visible light. A blank experiment without adding any photocatalyst was carried out. After exposure to sunlight, we withdrawal 5 mL sample and separated the catalysts by centrifugation of the suspension prior to evaluating the photodegradation. The concentrations of MG were determined by a UV–VIS spectrophotometer based on its absorbance at 617 nm. The MG removal effectively by CQDs, MXene and CQDs/MXene nanocomposite after 30 min irradiation under visible-light can be seen in Fig. 2 a. The results show that CQDs/MXene composites had significantly enhancement performance compared to pure MXene and CQDs.

The loading of CQDs in MXene nanosheet has a highly effective influence on their photocatalytic activity. The rate and percentage of MG degradation were significantly improved by the presence of CQDs and MXene. Fig 2b displays the color change of the MG dye resulting from photodegradation, clearly displaying the colorless solution after the completion of the photodegradation process.

To govern the optimal pH, experiments were conducted in the range of pH changes from 2.0 to 8.0, with a set initial concentration and contact time on a precisely measured quantity of adsorbent (6 mg). Fig. 3 a depicts the impact of initial pH on dye degradation. The surface binding sites and aqueous chemistry of the catalyst can be impacted by alterations in the pH [3]. In aqueous media, MG exists in its cationic form, thus interacting with negatively charged surfaces [32].The surface charge of the catalyst is essential in a photocatalytic reaction occurring on its surface. In this study, the use of KOH to cure Ti3AlC2 in the presence of little water facilitated the extraction of the Al layer, as OH groups replace the Al atoms during the etching process. A strong electrostatic reaction can be established between the dye and the catalyst via this process. The degradation percentages of MG increased continuously with the gradual increase of the pH, from 2 to 6. The addition of negative charges on the surface of the nanocomposite resulting in a higher electrostatic contact between the negatively charged functional groups of the composite and the positively charged MG molecules could be the reason behind this phenomenon. Therefore, a dye solution pH value of 6 was chosen as a fair value for further study.

To achieve and maintain the highest degradation efficiency, it is imperative to utilize the optimal amount of catalyst. Hence, in this study, the impact of CQDs/MXene amount on the photocatalytic reaction under visible light irradiation was investigated. At room temperature and pH = 6.0, the catalyst amount was varied in the range of 2 to 10 mg to degrade a 70 mg/L MG solution. The pertinent results are represented in Fig. 3b. As can be seen, 6 mg has good photodegradation activity on MG dye. Also, an increase in catalyst dosage leads to a substantial rise in the percentage of MG degradation. This is because the larger dosage results in a more extensive surface area, greater exposure of catalytic active sites, and enhanced catalyst adsorption capacity [26]. The pH and temperature of the initial solution were 6 and 24 °C, but in the final stage altered to 6.1 and 36 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the initial concentration of dye
ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between the degradation efficiency and the initial MG concentration was assessed through exposing 6 mg of the catalyst to different concentrations of MG ranging from 10 to 200 mg/L, followed by 25 min of light irradiation. It was seen that the initial MG concentration can highly impact the reduction percentage of the dye in the aqueous solution. The results reveal that the amount of dye degradation initially increases with the rise in the initial concentration of malachite green and then declines at high concentrations (200 mg/L) due to saturation on the photocatalyst's surface. The interaction of the substance molecules with the functional groups on the composite surface facilitate the absorption of dyes on the CQDs/MXene surface. As anticipated, the concentration gradient increases with the increase in dye concentration, thereby boosting the probability of the dye hitting the adsorbent particles. Thus, as long as the active centers of the adsorbent remain unsaturated, the rate of dye degradation increases. However, a decrease in dye degradation can be seen at high concentrations due to surface saturation, as reported in reference [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of irradiation time on photocatalysis reaction
ce:section.ce:para: How contact time affects the rate of deterioration was evaluated with CQDs/ MXene at an initial MG concentration of 75 mg L–1 under visible light, after the regular intervals of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 min of visible light irradiation. The final dye concentration in the solution was evaluated and the dye removal percentage was seen to increase with higher contact time. Fig. 3d, shows the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/ MXene photocatalyst in degrading MG under different times of visible light irradiation. The degradation of MG was done only within 25 min and the photodegradation percentages of the MG dye were calculated to be 98.6 % for CQDs/MXene. Therefore, by comparing this result with the previous results, we can conclude that the composite has enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to pure MXene and CQDs as photocatalyst. Subsequently, there was a nearly linear increase in degradation as reaction time progressed, albeit at a slower rate. This behavior is indicative of the influence of fresh catalyst and activated sites. Notably, CQDs/MXene exhibited a higher percentage of dye removal, which could be the result of synergistic effects of the high adsorption capacity of the composite and the rapid photo-induced charge transfer. The CQDs functioned as electron sinks, increasing the photocatalytic efficiency, recombination rates of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and activity of MXene. The superior performance of CQDs/MXene was compared to other recent reports involving CQDs composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic study of the MG degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on existing literature, it has been observed that a first-order reaction generally describes the kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of most organic pollutants under visible light irradiation. The rate constants for this reaction can be determined using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. (Eq. (2)) [34].
(2)
ln
(
C t
/
C 0
)
= k t
Where C0 is the dye solution's initial concentration (in mg/L); Ct displays the dye solution concentration over time (in min); and k is the dye's photodegradation's first order rate constant (in min 1). The rate constants were determined from the slope of the plots of the values of ln (Ct/C0) against time (in min), as shown in Fig. 4 b. The plots were linear. Under ideal circumstances, the dye degradation happened with the presence of pure CQDs, MXene and CQDs/MXene nanocomposite.

Plot of ln (Ct/C0) against t exhibit linear trends, depicting that the pseudo-first-order model can sufficiently describe the MG photodegradation process. The reaction rate constants for the samples were calculated from the slope of the fitted curve by means of linear regression and the estimated values were 0.0012 min−1, 0.0369 min−1 and 0.1382 min−1 for CQDs, MXene and CQDs/MXene, respectively. As concluded from the results, CQDs/MXene nanocomposite was able to enhance the photodegradation of the MG dye by 3.7 times compared to pure MXene.

The ability to reuse a catalyst is essential to minimize the cost associated with dye treatment so this study examined the stability and reusability of the prepared photocatalysts by washing the dye absorbent with 10.0 ml of deionized water. The results demonstrated that the adsorbent was reusable following this washing step. As a result, the catalyst was recovered from the reaction mixture, rinsed several times in deionized water, and dried, thus reused for more runs of dye photodegradation. The cyclic performance of the photocatalyst revealed 97.0 %, 95 %, and 47 % degradation of MG dye in the first, second, and third cycles, respectively, after 25 min of irradiation (Fig. 5 ).

The assessment of water quality relies significantly on the quantity of oxygen required to oxidize organic contaminants; a key factor measured by chemical oxygen demand (COD). COD provides specific insights into the amount of oxygen needed to mineralize organic components into CO2, H2O, etc. [29,30]. In this study, the potassium dichromate standard procedure was employed to determine the extent of mineralization of organic dye molecules. The findings revealed a substantial decrease in COD due to the application of CQDs/MXene photocatalyst during photodegradation. The degradation of COD was notably influenced by the irradiation time. As the exposure period increased, there was a proportional increase in both color and COD degradation. The percentage of dye degradation was particularly impacted by extended exposure times, leading to increased electron-hole formation and reduced electron-hole recombination. Significant results were observed, with 49 % and 78 % COD degradation achieved after 10 and 30 min of exposure, respectively, using a 100 ppm MG dye concentration. These outcomes highlight the effectiveness of the CQDs/MXene photocatalyst in facilitating COD reduction and the degradation of organic dye molecules under different exposure durations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for superior photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: The early stages of the interaction between light and materials involve photon absorption, dynamics trapping, surface trapping, and charge generation, whereas the later stages involve surface activity and the generation of surface radicals [35]. The combined effect of both CQDs and MXene in the nanocomposite is responsible for the improved photocatalytic activity of CQDs/MXene in degrading MG. When the visible light irradiates the nanocomposite, electrons (e −) in the valence band (VB) of MXene get excited to the conduction band (CB), which generates a hole (h +) in the VB. After that, the photogenerated electrons can be moved from MXene to CQDs. This electron transfer from MXene to CQDs helps to separate charges and prevents charge recombination, thus creating photo-reactive sites and enhancing optical absorption in the visible light range [36–38].

The electrons in the CB act as reducing agents, while the holes in the VB serve as oxidants. Therefore, the collected electrons on the surface of CQDs react with O2 molecules on the nanocomposite surface to generate radicals (O2 −•), such as superoxide anion radicals, subsequently generating OH• radicals. However, the holes generated in the VB can react with H2O or OH− to create strong oxidizing agents: OH• radicals. The O2 −• and OH• radicals play a crucial role in the further degradation of dye molecules. In addition, this nanocomposite creates a setting that effectively increases the surface active sites for the interaction between dye and semiconductor, which enhances the photo-decolorization processes (Eq. (3)) [39].
(3) CQDs/MXene + hν→ e −(CB) + h + (VB)
e − (CB) + O2 → O2 –•
O2 –• + H2O → H2O2 + O2
H2O2 → 2 OH•
h + (VB) + H2O → OH• + H +
MG + OH• → Degradation product (CO2+ H2O)

In Table 1 , other MG degrading catalysts previously reported were compared to CQDs/MXene in terms of their photocatalytic performance. This comparison considered different experimental parameters. The results of this comparison demonstrate that our novel nanocomposites display good photodegradation activity for dyes.

From the present investigation, it was clear that CQDs/MXene was effective low-cost and non-toxic materials for water remediation. The CQDs/MXene nanocomposites, with their impressive visible-light photocatalytic efficiency, hold potential as a candidate for the photocatalytic purification of wastewater under visible light exposure. The CQDs used in this study were produced through a green chemistry method using Petroselinum crispum leaves, while the mostly OH-terminated Ti3C2(OH)2 nanosheets were successfully prepared via a simple etching method using alkaline solution treatment (KOH diluted with some water). The CQDs/MXene were then prepared through a self-assembly process. Our findings demonstrate that the as-produced powder displayed excellent photocatalytic activity throughout three cycles of photodegradation. Moreover, the powder displayed an outstanding degradation efficiency of MG dye in the solution after 25 min under visible light.

Mir Mahdi Zahedi: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation. Somayeh Keyvani Samarin: Formal analysis, Methodology. Shiva Dehghan Abkenar: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Azam Bagheri Pebdeni: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Morteza Hosseini: Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shankramma Kalikeri: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hassan Karimi-Maleh: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
